sections.heading: Core Concept
sections.content: Merge Sort applies a **Divide and Conquer** strategy with an [[primary|easy split, hard merge]] characteristic. It recursively splits the array down to single elements, then focuses all complexity on the merge phase to assemble a fully sorted sequence.
items: The **Divide** phase [[primary|splits the array in half]] recursively until each sub-array holds a single element, which is trivially sorted
The **Conquer** phase is the algorithmic challenge: two sorted sub-arrays must be compared element by element and [[primary|merged]] into one larger sorted sequence
It is a **stable sort** (Stable Sorting), preserving the relative order of equal elements, at the cost of requiring extra [[primary|auxiliary space]] to hold intermediate results

sections.heading: Complexity
sections.content: Merge Sort's time complexity is [[success|O(n log n)]] in all three cases — best, average, and worst — regardless of input order. The recursion tree always has log n levels and each level merges a total of n elements, so all three cases are identical and unlike Quick Sort's worst-case degradation.
items: **All three cases are O(n log n)**: whether the input is sorted, reversed, or random, every level must merge all n elements across log n levels
**Split**: compute midpoint and slice — [[success|O(1)]] per level
**Merge**: traverse and compare elements — [[warning|O(n)]] per level
**Space**: non-in-place sort, requires [[warning|O(n)]] auxiliary memory; the recursive version adds an O(log n) call stack on top

sections.heading: What Can Be Optimized
sections.content: Merge Sort excels with **large datasets** and **linked lists**, and guarantees **stable sorting**. However, its extra memory requirement makes it unsuitable for memory-constrained environments or very small inputs. Python's `sorted()` uses **Timsort**, which first uses Insertion Sort to carve the array into small sorted segments (runs), then merges them with Merge Sort — getting the best of both.
items: Ideal for **large datasets** or **Linked Lists** — performance remains consistently stable
Ideal when **stable sorting** is required, preserving the original relative order of equal-valued elements
Not suitable for **memory-constrained** arrays; the extra space overhead means **Quick Sort** is often preferred

Not suitable for **very small inputs** (e.g., n < 64) — recursive merge overhead outweighs the benefit; **Insertion Sort** is better, and **Timsort** uses exactly this insight to combine both algorithms

sections.heading: When to Use It
sections.content: Whenever you spot clues like "linked list", "external data", or "stable sort", Merge Sort is the go-to solution. It also shines for advanced problems that involve counting relative-position changes among elements.
items: **Linked list sorting**: lists lack random access — Quick Sort's partition requires index-based positioning and degrades to O(n) on lists; Merge Sort only needs two forward-advancing pointers and can merge by modifying pointers without any temp array, reducing space to O(log n)

**Counting Inversions**: during merge, when `right[j] < left[i]`, since left is already sorted, every element from left[i] to the end of left forms an inversion pair with right[j] — batch-add `len(left) - i` at once, keeping the overall complexity at O(n log n)

**External sorting**: disk data cannot all fit in memory; Merge Sort's merge step naturally supports a streaming pattern — read two sorted blocks, write one sorted block — a perfect fit for sequential disk I/O
**Multi-key stable sorting**: when the problem demands O(n log n) efficiency and the original relative order of equal elements must be preserved exactly

sections.heading: Recursion Execution Order
sections.content: Diagrams often create the illusion that the array is split level by level all at once; in reality, **recursion** [[primary|always dives all the way down the left half first]] before it ever touches the right. For an array of size n=2^k, the recursion tree has **k+1 levels** in total (root through leaves) — e.g., n=8 gives 4 levels, n=4 gives 3 levels. An array of n elements produces exactly **n-1 merge calls** (one per internal node of the recursion tree).
items: Static teaching diagrams show **simultaneous level-by-level** splitting — a visual illusion that does not reflect how the program actually runs
The code follows the **left half** recursive call all the way down until the sub-array has only one element
Only after the left side bottoms out and completes its initial **merge** does the call stack unwind and begin processing the corresponding **right half**

The standard code template has three key parts: `if len(arr) <= 1: return arr` as the base case, `arr[:mid]` for the left half, `arr[mid:]` for the right half, then `merge(left, right)` to combine two sorted sub-arrays

Odd-length example: sorting `[3,1,2]` with mid=1, the left sub-array `[3]` returns immediately; the first merge call is `merge([1],[2])` inside the right sub-array `[1,2]`, not the outer `merge([3],[1,2])`

sections.heading: The Critical Merge Step
sections.content: Merge Sort's [[primary|easy split, hard merge]] nature means splitting is trivial, but correctly combining two sorted sub-arrays using **dual pointers** and **extra space** is the core implementation challenge where most beginners get stuck. The merge comparison must use `<=` (less than or equal): switching to strict `<` would prefer the right element on ties, breaking stable sort.
items: The actual sorting happens during the **Merge** phase — every element in both sub-arrays must be visited and compared
**Extra space** (a temporary array) is required to store the merged result; elements cannot be swapped in-place the way in-place sorts do
Implementation requires careful **dual-pointer** control, tracking progress in each sub-array and picking the smaller value into the temp buffer each step

Short trace example: in `merge([1,4], [2,3])`, the while loop appends 1, then 2, then 3; once the right half is exhausted, the remaining 4 is appended by `extend(left[i:])` instead of another comparison

When one sub-array is exhausted first, all remaining elements from the other sub-array must be appended to the end of the merged result; if `merge([1,2,3], [])` is called, the while loop is false immediately and the result becomes `[1,2,3]` from the remaining elements

sections.heading: Merge Sort vs Quick Sort
sections.content: Both Merge Sort and Quick Sort run in O(n log n) on average, but they differ fundamentally in worst-case behavior, stability, and space requirements.
items: **Time stability**: Merge Sort is O(n log n) in all three cases; Quick Sort can degrade to [[danger|O(n²)]] in the worst case when the pivot is always the extreme value and partitions are completely unbalanced
**Space**: Merge Sort requires O(n) auxiliary array; Quick Sort is nearly in-place, using only O(log n) recursion stack
**Stability**: Merge Sort is naturally stable; Quick Sort's partition swaps can change the relative order of equal-key elements, making it an **unstable sort**
**Practical choice**: prefer Merge Sort when memory is available and stability matters; prefer Quick Sort for better cache performance and in-place sorting
